_notes: Auto-generated by scripts/classify_districts.py (Stage 4) produced an EMPTY rule set: this pack's scraped breadcrumbs are only 1 level deep for EVERY one of its 80 sources -- metadata.breadcrumb is literally just ['Chapter 40.2 - ZONING'] with no article or division text at all (unlike fredericksburg-va/lynchburg-va/manassas-va, which had a real but shallow 2-3 level breadcrumb). So Stage 4's article+division grouping collapsed the entire 80-section pack into ONE group with article='' and division='', and correctly refused to assign a single district (confidence 0.5, rationale 'appear to be introductory or administrative in nature'). This is a genuinely different shape from the other three empty-rules-tier cities Stage 4 abstained on: Portsmouth's ordinance (a modern 2024-era UDO, Ch. 40.2) is NOT 'one section per individual district code' -- it is 'one section per DISTRICT CATEGORY,' where each base-district-family gets exactly one section containing ALL the codes in that family as lettered subsections (A)/(B)/(C)/... within a single citable chunk. Confirmed by reading manifest.json full_text for every source under Sec. 40.2-200 through 40.2-218 (all 80 sources are flat top-level entries in the sources array -- there is no further per-code chunking at ingestion time, no repeated section_ref, and Table 40.2-201 'Zoning Districts Established' independently confirms the category groupings: NR/GR/UR/UR-M=RESIDENTIAL, NMU/GMU/MU-H=COMMERCIAL, IL/IN=INDUSTRIAL, WF/C=SPECIAL, HR/HLO/HLB=HISTORIC SUB-DISTRICTS, D1 T3/T4/T5/T6/SD=DOWNTOWN SUB-DISTRICTS). Because the citable unit is the whole category, this pack uses ONE title_contains rule per section (there is no finer citable grain to key on, and article/division are empty for all 80 sources so article_contains/division_contains cannot be used at all -- every rule below relies on title_contains only, verified unique against all 80 section titles in the pack). Human-reviewed 2026-07-18. Mapping rationale per section: (1) Sec. 40.2-202 Residential Zoning Districts -> residential-low-density. Contains NR (Neighborhood Residential, single-family detached, low density), GR (General Residential, single-family/duplex/2-4-family, moderate density), UR (Urban Residential, mid-density traditional neighborhoods with some neighborhood-serving mixed use), UR-M (Multi-Family Urban Residential, high-density urban core, formerly 'UR-H' High Density Urban Residential). All four collapse to 'residential-low-density' regardless of density tier -- the closed DISTRICT_VOCAB has no separate high-density residential term, same precedent as every other VA pack in this rollout (fredericksburg/lynchburg/manassas all did the same for their R-2...R-30/R-1...R-7 spreads). (2) Sec. 40.2-204 Industrial Zoning Districts -> industrial-zone. Contains IL (Light Industrial, small-scale/low-impact) and IN (Industrial, heavy manufacturing/large-scale) -- unambiguous. (3) Sec. 40.2-203 Commercial Zoning Districts -> mixed-use-core, NOT commercial-employment, despite the section's own umbrella title saying 'Commercial.' JUDGMENT CALL: applying the same 'explicit residential+commercial mixing in the purpose text' test manassas-va used to sort its B-3/B-3.5 into mixed-use-core instead of commercial-employment (see manassas-va classification_rules.json _notes), all three codes in this section fail the 'pure commercial, no residential mixing' test that WAS satisfied by manassas's B-1/B-2/B-4: NMU (Neighborhood Mixed-Use) purpose text reads 'accommodate a mix of residential and small-scale... retail and service uses'; GMU (General Mixed-Use) reads 'encourage the formation and continuance of a compatible mixed-use environment that includes residential, commercial, and institutional uses'; MU-H (High Intensity Mixed-Use) reads 'accommodate a mix of residential and a diverse range of medium to high intensity retail, service, and office uses.' All three districts are literally named '___ Mixed-Use' in Table 40.2-201, not '___ Commercial' -- the section's own umbrella heading ('Commercial Zoning Districts') is the ordinance's category-table label, but every individual district's own purpose text and name says mixed-use. Deferring to the individual-district language (as manassas-va's precedent did) over the umbrella category label, this section maps to mixed-use-core. This means Portsmouth has NO section mapped to 'commercial-employment' -- there is no pure office/retail-only district family in this ordinance; every non-residential, non-industrial district here explicitly mixes in residential use. (4) Sec. 40.2-207 Downtown D1 District and Sub-Districts -> mixed-use-core. Contains five sub-districts (T3 Urban Residential, T4 General Urban, T5 Urban Center, T6 Urban Core, SD Special District) using explicit urbanist-transect language: T3 'contains a mix of residential uses,' T4 'contains a mix of uses, but maintains a predominantly residential urban fabric,' T5 'a higher density mix of uses with primarily retail space permitted on the ground floor,' T6 'high-density and waterfront mixed uses... the most intense land use Downtown.' Four of five sub-districts are unambiguous mixed-use-core. MINOR CAVEAT (not enough to leave the section unmapped): the fifth sub-district, SD ('Special District'), is described as 'a low-intensity, limited industrial mixed-use district... including manufacturing, distribution and warehousing, as well as civic uses and retail uses' -- more industrial-tinged than T3-T6, but its own text still calls it a 'mixed-use district,' and it is one paragraph out of five within a section whose general-purposes preamble and 4/5 sub-districts are dominantly mixed-use-core/urbanist-transect in character, so the section as a whole was kept as mixed-use-core rather than left unmapped. (5) Sec. 40.2-216 Use Table and Sec. 40.2-218 Area, Bulk, Density, Building, and Setback Standards -> both are citywide standards tables spanning every mapped district column (NR GR UR UR-M NMU GMU MU-H IL IN D1-T3...T6 D1-SD, plus HR/HLO/HLB/C/WF which are NOT separately mapped -- see below), analogous to fredericksburg-va's 'Use Table' citywide rule -- mapped to every vocabulary term actually used elsewhere in this pack: residential-low-density, mixed-use-core, industrial-zone (no commercial-employment or agricultural, since neither term is used by any other rule in this pack). HARD JUDGMENT CALL, LEFT UNMAPPED: Sec. 40.2-205 Special Zoning Districts was deliberately NOT mapped to any vocabulary term. It contains exactly two codes with genuinely incompatible characters inside one citable chunk: WF (Waterfront) passes the same mixed-use test as Sec. 40.2-203/207 with even stronger language ('Encourage development of high quality, mixed-use residential, retail, office, or hotel development... Integrate commercial and residential uses horizontally at the street level and vertically within buildings') and would cleanly map to mixed-use-core in isolation; but C (Conservation, formerly 'Preservation/Government') is the opposite -- a pure environmental-protection/open-space district ('conserve watercourses... retain open spaces and greenways... conserve wildlife and plant life habitats') with minimal buildable standards (43,560 sq ft min lot, 5% max building coverage, no stated max density) that does not read as 'agricultural' (no farming/agricultural-activity language, unlike manassas-va's A-1) or fit any other vocabulary term. Since WF and C are roughly equal-weight sub-districts within the same section and pull toward genuinely different terms, forcing either one onto the whole section would misrepresent the other -- so, matching the 'don't force heterogeneous groups into a single vocab term' precedent that is the entire premise of Stage 4's abstention behavior, this section was left unmapped (defaults to unknown/general) rather than guessed. LEFT UNMAPPED (unknown/general), all deliberate: Sec. 40.2-200 (General Provisions, about the districts article, not a district itself) and Sec. 40.2-201 (Zoning Districts Established -- an index/enumeration table of every district, no operative development standards of its own); Sec. 40.2-206 Historic Districts (HR/HLO/HLB sub-districts plus COA/material-change-review procedures -- a regulatory-review overlay layered on top of base districts, matching the 'leave historic overlays unmapped' precedent from every prior VA pack); Sec. 40.2-208 Overlay Zoning Districts (MP Master-Planned, DD Downtown Design, I Innovation, E Entertainment, CBPA Chesapeake Bay Preservation Area, FP Floodplain -- textbook overlays superimposed on underlying base districts, explicitly described as such in the section's own general-purpose text); and the entire 40.2-100s (short title/authority/purpose/severability/zoning map/fees -- administrative), 40.2-209 through 40.2-215 and 40.2-217 (use-category definitions and use-specific standards organized by USE, not by district -- cross-district topical sections, same as how fredericksburg-va/manassas-va left their per-use-category articles unmapped), and the 40.2-300s/400s/500s ranges (off-street parking/landscaping/signage/lighting standards, nonconforming uses/structures/lots, administration/BZA/HPC, applications/permits/variances/rezoning, site plan review, enforcement -- all genuinely cross-district standards and procedure articles, not base zoning districts). Rules are first-match.

rules.title_contains: area, bulk, density, building, and setback standards
